answer.rationale: Apparatus and concordance traversal of plēroō (G4137) and plērōma (G4138) demonstrate Christ filling all things by divine prerogative and ecclesial headship, not by spatial extension of the human body. The canonical lexical pattern of analambanō (G353), anabainō (G305), kathizō (G2523), and dexia (G1188) locates the resurrected body in a particular heavenly throne-room until the visible return (hon tropon, Acts 1:11). Col 2:9 plērōma tēs theotētos sōmatikōs (G4138 G2320 G4985) places the divine fullness IN the incarnate body without entailing the body's extension everywhere. Luke 24:39 sarka kai ostea (G4561 G3747) and Acts 1:11 etheasasthe poreuomenon (G2300 G4198) name the body as a circumscribed, observable entity. The Lutheran inference (genus maiestaticum extended to ubiquity) is not entailed by the lexical evidence; it is a theological inference layered on the communicatio idiomatum to support eucharistic real-presence.
answer.notes: This is an in-house Christian theological dispute between Lutheran (Luther, Brenz, Formula of Concord 1577 Article VIII) and Reformed (Calvin, the extra Calvinisticum, Westminster, Heidelberg) traditions over whether the communicatio idiomatum extends divine omnipresence to Christ's human nature. The Lutheran motive is eucharistic: ubiquity grounds real-presence in, with, and under the bread. The Reformed counter is that a true human body, by Chalcedonian rule, cannot be ubiquitous without confusion of natures. The Chalcedonian definition (asynchutōs, atreptōs, adiairetōs, achōristōs) holds that each nature retains its own properties; ubiquity arguably violates the asynchutōs clause. Eastern Orthodox, Catholic, Anglican (39 Articles Art. 28-29), Methodist (Articles XVIII), Reformed, Anabaptist, and Pentecostal traditions all hold Christ's body is locally in heaven until his return. Lutheranism stands alone among historic confessional families in affirming bodily ubiquity. The doctrine is therefore Lutheran-distinctive, not a Christological boundary. Denial of ubiquity is not denial of Christ's deity, incarnation, resurrection, ascension, or session at the right hand. Reformed Christians who deny ubiquity remain fully Trinitarian and Chalcedonian. The verdict on the proposition as stated (Christ's glorified human body IS ubiquitous) is no, on canonical-lexical grounds. The Brethren formation inherits the broadly Reformed-evangelical reading; this baseline confirms that the Reformed reading is canonically defensible while the Lutheran reading requires a theological inference beyond the apparatus. Carriers: Martin Luther, Johannes Brenz, Martin Chemnitz, the Formula of Concord (Solid Declaration VIII).

stem_audit.neutralized_form: Does the union of Christ's human and divine natures (the communicatio idiomatum) entail that his glorified human body shares the divine attribute of omnipresence and is therefore present everywhere?
stem_audit.notes: The original statement uses Lutheran-Reformed disputation vocabulary ('ubiquitous', 'by virtue of its union with the divine nature') that telegraphs the Lutheran position. The neutralized form puts the live question (does the communicatio idiomatum extend omnipresence to the human nature?) on the table without smuggling the verdict. Flag stem-pre-loaded-verdict applied.

lay_summary.reasoning: The question is whether the human body of the risen Jesus is everywhere at once, the same way God is everywhere at once. The answer from Scripture is no. The Bible names two things together. First, Jesus has a real human body with flesh and bones (Luke 24:39, where the risen Jesus says, touch me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have). Second, that body ascended and is now in a particular heavenly place. Acts 1:11 says, this same Jesus who was taken up from you into heaven will come back in the same way you saw him go. The wording 'in the same way' assumes Jesus left visibly from one place and will return visibly to one place. Hebrews 1:3 says he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high, which names a specific seat, not everywhere. The strongest argument FOR the proposition is Ephesians 1:23 and 4:10, which say Christ fills all things, and Colossians 2:9, which says the fullness of deity dwells in him bodily. These verses do say Christ in his whole person fills the universe and that divine fullness lives in the incarnate Christ. But they do not say his human body is stretched across the universe. They say his divine nature, which has always filled all things, is now joined to a human nature in one person, and through that one person he fills, rules, and is present to his church. Matthew 28:20, I am with you always, is a promise of personal presence through the Spirit, not a claim that his physical body is in every place. The strongest complicating text the position has to handle is Acts 1:11 plus Luke 24:39 plus Acts 3:21, which says heaven must receive him until the time of restoration. Heaven receives him; he is not bodily everywhere. The verdict handles the tension by separating what is true of Christ as a whole person (he fills all things, he is with us always, he is sovereign over all) from what is true of his human body specifically (it is the one risen body that the apostles touched, that was taken up, and that will return visibly).
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: The Lutheran tradition is the historic confessional home of this teaching. Luther developed it in the 1520s during his eucharistic debates with Zwingli at Marburg. Johannes Brenz codified it. The Formula of Concord (1577), Article VIII on the Person of Christ, teaches the communicatio idiomatum with a genus maiestaticum, where divine majesty including omnipresence is communicated to the human nature of the glorified Christ. Every other major historic Christian tradition rejects bodily ubiquity in this form. The patristic and Chalcedonian tradition holds that each nature in Christ retains its own properties (the four adverbs: without confusion, without change, without division, without separation). The Catholic Catechism teaches the ascension as the entrance of Christ's humanity into God's heavenly domain, from which he will come again, hidden in the meantime. Eastern Orthodoxy explicitly rejects the Lutheran model, teaching a mystical sacramental presence rather than ubiquity of the body. The Anglican 39 Articles (Article 28 on the Lord's Supper, Article 29) reject the corporal presence and locate Christ's body in heaven. The Wesleyan-Methodist Articles of Religion Article XVIII say the body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the Supper only after a heavenly and spiritual manner. The Reformed family (Westminster Confession 29, Heidelberg Catechism Lord's Day 28, Belgic Confession Article 35) reject ubiquity on the basis of the extra Calvinisticum: the divine nature of Christ is not contained within the human nature, so the divine omnipresence is not transferred to the human body. Anabaptist and Pentecostal traditions follow the broader Reformed pattern. This is not a cult-marker doctrine. Reformed Christians who deny ubiquity are mainstream Christians. Lutherans who affirm it are mainstream Christians. The denial of ubiquity does not place anyone outside historic Christianity; the affirmation does not either. It is a Lutheran-distinctive teaching arising from a particular christological premise about how the two natures share properties after the glorification.

scripture.key_term: ἐξουσία exousia G1849 (authority); δίδωμι didōmi G1325 (given, edothē aorist passive); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; γῆ gē G1093
scripture.force: All authority (exousia) in heaven and on earth has been given (edothē) to me. The text grants pan-cosmic authority to the risen Christ but says nothing about spatial extension of the human body. Authority is a relational-juridical category, not a metaphysical claim about bodily location.

scripture.key_term: ἐγώ egō G1473 (I); μετά meta G3326 (with); εἰμί eimi G1510 (am); ἡμέρα hēmera G2250 (days)
scripture.force: I am with you (egō meth' hymōn eimi) all the days until the consummation of the age. The promise is personal presence through the Spirit (cf. John 14:16-18, John 16:7), not bodily ubiquity. The same Gospel that says he ascended (Mark 16:19 implicit) and sat at the right hand grounds the promise of presence in the sending of the Paraclete and the personal-relational meta-with formula. Reading this as bodily ubiquity creates a contradiction with Acts 1:11 within the same Lukan corpus.

scripture.key_term: πλήρωμα plērōma G4138 (fullness); πληρόω plēroō G4137 (fills, plēroumenou middle participle); πᾶς pas G3956 (all); κεφαλή kephalē G2776 (head); σῶμα sōma G4983 (body)
scripture.force: He put all things under his feet and gave him as head (kephalē) over all things to the church, which is his body (sōma), the fullness (plērōma) of him who fills all in all (tou ta panta en pasin plēroumenou). The 'body' here is the CHURCH, not the human flesh of Jesus, which makes a body-ubiquity reading lexically awkward. The participle plēroumenou is middle, meaning Christ fills the cosmos for himself, but the noun plērōma is the church being filled. Lexically: Christ fills all things in his role as cosmic head; the church is the recipient and embodied locus of that filling. The verse does not lexically isolate the human body as the agent of universal filling.

scripture.key_term: ἀναβαίνω anabainō G305 (ascended, anabas); ὑπεράνω hyperanō G5231 (far above); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; πληρόω plēroō G4137 (might fill, plērōsē aorist subjunctive)
scripture.force: He who descended is the same who ascended (anabas) far above (hyperanō) all the heavens, that he might fill (plērōsē) all things. The ascension is the means of the filling. Hyperanō pantōn tōn ouranōn names a specific upward direction and a definite endpoint, not a diffusion across all space. The hina-clause names cosmic filling as the purpose of the localized ascent. Lutheran reading: post-ascension the human nature now shares divine omnipresence. Reformed reading: post-ascension Christ in his whole person rules the cosmos through his Spirit and his word while his body remains seated at the right hand.

scripture.key_term: πλήρωμα plērōma G4138 (fullness); θεότης theotēs G2320 (deity); κατοικέω katoikeō G2730 (dwells); σωματικῶς sōmatikōs G4985 (bodily)
scripture.force: In him (Christ) all the fullness (pan to plērōma) of deity (theotētos) dwells (katoikei, present indicative) bodily (sōmatikōs). The verse asserts that the divine fullness resides IN the incarnate body, not that the body itself fills the universe. The directionality is into the body, not out from the body. Sōmatikōs functions adverbially to specify the mode of indwelling: corporeally, in real human embodiment. The Lutheran extension from this verse to bodily ubiquity is an inference, not a lexical entailment.

scripture.key_term: ἐπαίρω epairō G1869 (lifted up, epērthē aorist passive); νεφέλη nephelē G3507 (cloud); ὑπολαμβάνω hypolambanō G5274 (received); θεάομαι theaomai G2300 (beheld); τρόπος tropos G5158 (manner); ἔρχομαι erchomai G2064 (will come)
scripture.force: While they were looking on (blepontōn autōn) he was lifted up (epērthē) and a cloud received him (hypelaben) from their sight. This same Jesus (houtos ho Iēsous) will come (eleusetai) in the same manner (hon tropon) as you have beheld (etheasasthe) him going (poreuomenon) into heaven. The text names the ascension as a particularized, localized departure from one place and the return as a particularized arrival. If the body were ubiquitous post-glorification, the angelic interpretation 'in the same manner' loses lexical force. The body is taken from sight, not stretched across space.

scripture.key_term: δέχομαι dechomai G1209 (receive, dexasthai aorist infinitive middle); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; ἀποκατάστασις apokatastasis G605 (restoration); χρόνος chronos G5550 (times)
scripture.force: Whom heaven must receive (hon dei ouranon men dexasthai) until the times of restoration (apokatastaseōs) of all things. Peter at the temple says heaven is currently holding Christ, until a future restoration. The verb dechomai with the impersonal dei marks heaven as the locating receptacle of the ascended body. If the body were ubiquitous, the language of heaven 'receiving' and 'until' becomes empty. Reformed appeal to this verse as locating Christ's body in heaven is lexically straightforward.

scripture.key_term: σάρξ sarx G4561 (flesh); ὀστέον osteon G3747 (bone); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151 (spirit); ψηλαφάω psēlaphaō G5584 (touch)
scripture.force: See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself. Touch me (psēlaphēsate) and see, for a spirit (pneuma) does not have flesh (sarka) and bones (ostea) as you see I have. The risen Christ defines his post-resurrection body as a touchable, observable, circumscribed human body. A ubiquitous body cannot be touched by Thomas (John 20:27) or eat fish (Luke 24:42-43) in any meaningful sense. The lexical force of sarx and osteon is concrete physicality, the opposite of the diffuse extension a strict bodily ubiquity would require.

scripture.key_term: σάρξ sarx G4561 (flesh); ὠφελέω ōpheleō G5623 (profits); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151 (spirit); ζωή zōē G2222 (life)
scripture.force: The flesh (sarx) profits nothing; it is the spirit (pneuma) that gives life. The words I have spoken to you are spirit and life. Jesus, after the bread-of-life discourse that the eucharistic-realist tradition reads as a sacramental promise, interprets his own teaching: physical flesh as such does not save; the spirit and the word do. This complicates a literal-physical eucharistic eating that the ubiquity doctrine is engineered to support.

scripture.key_term: καθίζω kathizō G2523 (sat down, ekathisen aorist); δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand); μεγαλωσύνη megalōsynē G3172 (Majesty); ὑψηλά hypsēla G5308 (on high)
scripture.force: After making purification for sins, he sat down (ekathisen) at the right hand of the Majesty on high. The seating language is repeated across Heb 1:13, 8:1, 10:12, 12:2 and presupposes a particular location. Sitting cannot be everywhere; it is a localized posture. The author of Hebrews treats Christ's enthronement as a definite event in a definite place.

scripture.key_term: εἰσέρχομαι eiserchomai G1525 (entered, eisēlthen aorist); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; ἐμφανίζω emphanizō G1718 (to appear)
scripture.force: Christ entered (eisēlthen) not into holy places made with hands but into heaven itself, now to appear (emphanisthēnai) in the presence of God on our behalf. The verb eiserchomai with eis ton ouranon names a definite movement into a definite location. The high-priestly typology requires Christ to be in the heavenly sanctuary, not extended through all space.

scripture.key_term: συμφέρω sympherō G4851 (expedient); ἀπέρχομαι aperchomai G565 (go away); παράκλητος paraklētos G3875 (Helper); πέμπω pempō G3992 (send)
scripture.force: It is to your advantage that I go away (apelthō); for if I do not go away, the Helper will not come to you. But if I go, I will send him to you. Jesus distinguishes his bodily departure from the Spirit's coming. If Christ's body were ubiquitous, the going-away and the sending-of-the-Spirit lose their logic. The mode of his continuing presence post-ascension is the Spirit, not the body.

scripture.key_term: πολίτευμα politeuma G4175 (citizenship); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; σωτήρ sōtēr G4990 (savior); μετασχηματίζω metaschēmatizō G3345 (transform); σῶμα sōma G4983 (body)
scripture.force: Our citizenship is in heaven, from which we await a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ, who will transform (metaschēmatisei) our lowly body to be like his glorious body. Christ comes FROM heaven (apo ouranou) to us. The future apo-clause names a particular point of origin. The glorified body is a definite body that ours will be conformed to, not a diffuse cosmic extension.

scripture.key_term: σῶμα sōma G4983 (body); πνευματικός pneumatikos G4152 (spiritual); ψυχικός psychikos G5591 (natural); εἰκών eikōn G1504 (image)
scripture.force: Paul's resurrection-body theology: sown a natural body (sōma psychikon), raised a spiritual body (sōma pneumatikon). Pneumatikon does not mean ubiquitous or immaterial; it means animated by the Holy Spirit, glorified, imperishable. The body remains a sōma, a circumscribed body, after the resurrection. Paul never extends sōma to a property of omnipresence.

scripture.key_term: ἄχρι achri G891 (until); ἔρχομαι erchomai G2064 (comes)
scripture.force: As often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until (achri) he comes (elthē). The temporal achri-clause assumes Christ is currently absent in bodily presence and the Supper is the proclamation that holds until he returns. If the body were ubiquitously present in every Supper as the Lutheran doctrine teaches, the until-he-comes language becomes lexically odd.

scripture.key_term: οἰκία oikia G3614 (house); τόπος topos G5117 (place); ἑτοιμάζω hetoimazō G2090 (prepare); πάλιν palin G3825 (again); παραλαμβάνω paralambanō G3880 (take)
scripture.force: In my Father's house are many rooms; I go to prepare a place (topon) for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again (palin erchomai) and take you (paralēmpsomai) to myself. Christ names a definite place to which he goes and from which he returns. The going and coming language is location-bound. Ubiquity would collapse the distinction between his going to prepare a place and his coming again to take the disciples.

scripture.key_term: מָלֵא male H4390 (fill); שָׁמַיִם shamayim H8064 (heavens); אֶרֶץ erets H776 (earth); נְאֻם ne'um H5002 (declaration)
scripture.force: Do I not fill (male) heaven and earth? declares the LORD. Divine omnipresence is named as a property of God in his deity, not of any creaturely body. The lexical foundation for omnipresence in the OT is theological, not somatic. The Lutheran inference must claim that what is true of YHWH essentially becomes true of the incarnate human nature; the Reformed reply is that this confuses the two natures by transferring an incommunicable divine property to the human nature.

scripture.key_term: אָנָה anah H575 (whither); רוּחַ ruach H7307 (Spirit); שָׁאוֹל sheol H7585; כָּנָף kanaph H3671 (wing)
scripture.force: Where shall I go from your Spirit (ruach)? Or where shall I flee from your presence? If I ascend to heaven, you are there; if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there. Omnipresence belongs to God's Spirit and presence as God. The OT lexical pattern grounds omnipresence in divine essence, not in any bodily-creaturely substrate.

competing_lens_verdicts.verdict: affirms ubiquity. Reads Col 2:9 plērōma sōmatikōs and Matt 28:18-20 as the human nature post-glorification sharing divine omnipresence so that real-presence in the Eucharist is grounded in the body's actual presence wherever the bread is.

denies ubiquity. Reads Acts 1:11, Acts 3:21, Luke 24:39 as locating the body in heaven; the divine nature is not contained within (extra) the human nature; the human nature retains its creaturely finitude per Chalcedon's asynchutōs clause.

complicates ubiquity. Chalcedon affirms each nature retains its own properties without confusion or change. Bodily ubiquity arguably violates the asynchutōs adverb. Most patristic and Catholic readings hold the body localized in heaven while the divine person fills all things.

hermeneutics.notes: The question is irreducibly a christological-hermeneutical dispute over how the communicatio idiomatum operates after the glorification. Lutheranism's genus maiestaticum extends divine majesty including omnipresence to the human nature. Reformed and the broader patristic tradition maintain that the two natures share properties through the person without one nature acquiring the essential properties of the other. The apparatus does not adjudicate the metaphysical question directly, but the canonical lexical pattern of location-language for the post-ascension body (kathizō, dexia, en hypsēlois, dechomai ouranon, hon tropon) is on the side of localized-body. The Lutheran reading is a theological inference from Col 2:9 and Matt 28:18-20 that requires reading 'fullness' and 'with you always' as bodily rather than personal-divine. Grammatico-historical reading of these passages does not lexically require the Lutheran inference.

counter_witness.anchor: Chalcedonian Definition (451 AD), CCEL Schaff Creeds of Christendom Vol 2
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ to be acknowledged in two natures, inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably; the distinction of natures being preserved, the property of each nature being preserved.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church 659-667 (Article 6, He Ascended into Heaven)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ's Ascension marks the definitive entrance of Jesus' humanity into God's heavenly domain, whence he will come again, but which in the meantime hides him from the eyes of men.

counter_witness.anchor: Formula of Concord, Solid Declaration VIII (Of the Person of Christ), bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Wherever Christ according to the divinity is, there He is also naturally and personally as the one undivided person; the human nature shares the divine majesty including omnipresence.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article IV Of the Resurrection of Christ and Article XXVIII Of the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ ascended into Heaven, and there sitteth, until he return to judge all men at the last day; the Body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the Supper, only after an heavenly and spiritual manner.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 29 paragraph 7 on the Lord's Supper; Heidelberg Catechism Lord's Day 28 Q&A 76-78; Belgic Confession Article 35
counter_witness.key_phrase: The body and blood of Christ are then not corporally or carnally in, with, or under the bread and wine; yet as really, but spiritually, present to the faith of believers; Christ's body is in heaven.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article XVIII Of the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: The body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the Supper, only after a heavenly and spiritual manner. And the mean whereby the body of Christ is received and eaten in the Supper is faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), Article X on the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Supper is a memorial of the suffering and death of Christ; the body of Christ is in heaven, the bread and cup are signs to faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith Volume II, The Sacraments: Holy Eucharist (oca.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Orthodoxy teaches a mystical sacramental presence that defies analysis and explanation in purely rational and logical terms; it is neither a merely symbolic commemoration nor a rationalistic physical presence.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths (ag.org); typical AG pastoral teaching on the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord's Supper is a memorial and proclamation of Christ's death until he comes; Christ is bodily ascended to the right hand of the Father and will return in the same way.

counter_witness.anchor: General Pentecostal and evangelical-charismatic teaching following the AG and broader Reformed-evangelical pattern
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ's body is bodily seated at the right hand of the Father and will return visibly; the Spirit, not bodily ubiquity, is the mode of Christ's continuing presence with his church.

web.quote: Eph 1:23: plērōma (G4138) of him who fills (plēroumenou G4137) all in all (ta panta en pasin). The 'body' is the church; the filling agent is the head.
Eph 4:10: anabas (G305) hyperanō (G5231) pantōn tōn ouranōn hina plērōsē (G4137) ta panta. Ascent is the means; filling is the purpose.
Col 2:9: pan to plērōma (G4138) tēs theotētos (G2320) katoikei (G2730) sōmatikōs (G4985). Fullness dwells in the body; the body is the locus, not the agent of cosmic extension.
Luke 24:39: sarka kai ostea (G4561 G3747) hōs eme theōreite echonta. The risen body is touchable, with flesh and bones, distinguished from a spirit.
Acts 1:11: houtos ho Iēsous ho analēmphtheis (G353) eleusetai hon tropon (G5158) etheasasthe (G2300) auton poreuomenon (G4198). Manner of going is the manner of returning.
Acts 3:21: hon dei ouranon men dexasthai (G1209) achri chronōn apokatastaseōs (G605) pantōn. Heaven receives him until the restoration.
Plēroō G4137 in Eph 1:23, 4:10: Christ fills the universe with his presence, power, activity. The lexicon does not isolate the human body as the agent.
Sōmatikōs G4985: bodily, corporeally; modifies how the fullness of deity dwells. The direction is into the body, not from the body outward.
Formula of Concord SD VIII: wherever Christ according to the divinity is, there He is also naturally and personally as the one undivided person. The Lutheran ubiquity ground.
Methodist Article XVIII: the body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the Supper, only after a heavenly and spiritual manner. The mean is faith.
OCA: Orthodoxy teaches a mystical sacramental presence; not Lutheran bodily ubiquity. The presence defies rational analysis but is not bodily extension.
Chalcedon 451: two natures inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably; the property of each nature being preserved. Ubiquity arguably violates inconfusedly.
